perturbation.original_text: Within [***] ([***]) days of the achievement of any such milestone, Bioeq shall invoice the relevant milestone amount to Licensee and Licensee shall remit payment to Bioeq within [***] ([***]) days upon receipt of Bioeq's invoice relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Within [***] ([***]) days of the achievement of any such milestone, Bioeq shall invoice the relevant milestone amount to Licensee and Licensee shall remit payment to Bioeq within 90 days upon receipt of Bioeq's invoice relating thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from a shorter, unspecified period to 90 days may violate state prompt payment laws, particularly if the contract is performed in a state with stricter requirements for invoice payments.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Act - Cal. Pub. Contract Code § 10262.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Prompt Payment Act requires state agencies to pay undisputed invoices within 45 days for public works contracts. While this contract may not be directly with a state agency, setting a 90-day payment term could be deemed unreasonable and contradict the spirit of prompt payment principles, potentially leading to disputes over timely compensation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1100 - 22355] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) PART 2. CONTRACTING BY STATE AGENCIES [10100 - 19150] ( Heading of Part 2 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. State Contract Act [10100 - 10285.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) ARTICLE 8. Modifications; Performance; Payment [10250 - 10265] ( Article 8 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) 10262.5. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay to any subcontractor, not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment, the respective amounts allowed the contractor on account of the work performed by the subcontractors, to the extent of each subcontractor’s interest therein. In the event that there is a good faith dispute over all or any portion of the amount due on a progress payment from the prime contractor or subcontractor to a subcontractor, then the prime contractor or subcontractor may withhold no more than 150 percent of the disputed amount. Any contractor who violates this section shall pay to the subcontractor a penalty of 2 percent of the amount due per month for every month that payment is not made. In any action for the collection of funds wrongfully withheld, the prevaili
perturbation.justification: The changed text sets a payment deadline of 90 days, while the Perturbation Explanation suggests this may violate state prompt payment laws. The Law Explanation cites California's Prompt Payment Act, requiring state agencies to pay undisputed invoices within 45 days for public works contracts. Although the contract may not be directly with a state agency, the explanation argues that a 90-day term could be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, PCC 10262.5, mandates that a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay any subcontractor not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment. While this snippet specifically addresses payments to subcontractors, it highlights the legal emphasis on prompt payment. The changed text's 90-day payment term directly contradicts the spirit and intent of prompt payment principles as reflected in both the Law Explanation and the Scraped Law Snippet, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall maintain, and shall ensure that its Affiliates maintain, records, in sufficient detail, which shall be complete and accurate and shall fully and properly reflect all Net Sales and Gross Margins indicated in the quarterly reports described in Section 7.3.2. For each quarterly report, Licensee shall maintain records reflecting the Net Sales and Gross Margins contained in such quarterly report for [***] ([***]) years following the date that such quarterly report is delivered to Bioeq.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall maintain, and shall ensure that its Affiliates maintain, records, in sufficient detail, which shall be complete and accurate and shall fully and properly reflect all Net Sales and Gross Margins indicated in the quarterly reports described in Section 7.3.2. For each quarterly report, Licensee shall maintain records reflecting the Net Sales and Gross Margins contained in such quarterly report for 1 year following the date that such quarterly report is delivered to Bioeq.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to 1 year could conflict with IRS regulations and other state laws which often require companies to keep financial records for at least 3-7 years for tax and audit purposes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns
perturbation.law_explanation: IRS regulation 26 U.S. Code § 6001 gives the IRS authority to specify record-keeping requirements and the duration of how long those records must be kept. The IRS generally recommends keeping records for 3 years from when you filed your original return or 2 years from the date you paid the tax, whichever date is later.

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from an unspecified duration ([***]) to a fixed 1 year. The perturbation explanation points out that this might conflict with IRS regulations requiring 3-7 years of record-keeping for tax purposes. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 (26 USC 6001) confirm the IRS's authority to set record-keeping requirements. This creates a contradiction because the contract's one-year retention period could lead to non-compliance with IRS regulations if they mandate a longer retention period.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever, the Receiving Party shall cease all use of and return to the Disclosing Party, or destroy, as the Disclosing Party shall specify in writing promptly upon such expiration or termination, all materials transferred pursuant to Section 11.4 and all copies of all documents and other materials that contain or embody any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, except to the extent that the Receiving Party is required by Applicable Laws to retain such documents, and provided further that each Party may keep copies of all Confidential Information within its ordinary legal archives (including IT back-up systems). Within [***] ([***]) days after the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall furnish the Disclosing Party with a certificate, duly executed by an officer of the Receiving Party, confirming that the Receiving Party has complied with its obligations under this Section 11.4.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever, the Receiving Party shall cease all use of and return to the Disclosing Party, or destroy, as the Disclosing Party shall specify in writing promptly upon such expiration or termination, all materials transferred pursuant to Section 11.4 and all copies of all documents and other materials that contain or embody any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, except to the extent that the Receiving Party is required by Applicable Laws to retain such documents, and provided further that each Party may keep copies of all Confidential Information within its ordinary legal archives (including IT back-up systems). Within 5 days after the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall furnish the Disclosing Party with a certificate, duly executed by an officer of the Receiving Party, confirming that the Receiving Party has complied with its obligations under this Section 11.4.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe to furnish a certificate confirming compliance with confidentiality obligations to just 5 days is unreasonably short. Depending on the volume and nature of confidential information, a longer period might be necessary, and is often legally implied to allow for proper fulfillment of the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 12a specifies how to compute time when a deadline falls on a holiday. By setting a 5-day period, with no allowance for such contingencies, this creates a risk of non-compliance if a holiday or weekend occurs near the termination date, contradicting the provisions that exist under the law for deadlines and legal compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 12a. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS ( Preliminary Provisions enacted 1872. ) 12a. (a) If the last day for the performance of any act provided or required by law to be performed within a specified period of time is a holiday, then that period is hereby extended to and including the next day that is not a holiday. For purposes of this section, “holiday” means all day on Saturdays, all holidays specified in Section 135 and, to the extent provided in Section 12b, all days that by terms of Section 12b are required to be considered as holidays. (b) This section applies to Sections 659, 659a, and 921, and to all other provisions of law providing or requiring an act to be performed on a particular day or within a specified period of time, whether expressed in this or any other code or statute, ordinance, rule, or regulation. (Amended by Stats. 2007, Ch. 263, Sec. 3. Effective January 1, 2008.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract by reducing the timeframe for the Receiving Party to furnish a certificate of compliance from an unspecified number of days (represented by '[***] ([***])') to 5 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that this timeframe might be insufficient, especially if holidays or weekends occur near the termination date. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically California Code of Civil Procedure § 12a, states that if the last day for performing an act required by law falls on a holiday, the period is extended to the next day that is not a holiday. By setting a strict 5-day deadline, the modified contract fails to account for this legal provision, potentially leading to non-compliance and a direct contradiction of established legal procedures for time computation.
